title: Glechoma lagopsis
intro: Glechoma lagopsis, also known as gypsophila or creeping gypsywort, is a perennial plant in the mint family (Lamiaceae) native to Europe. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its antispasmodic, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, respiratory problems, and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Glechoma lagopsis include phenolic acids, flavonoids, and volatile oils, including borneol, bornyl acetate, and limonene, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Glechoma lagopsis include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including digestive problems, anxiety, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Glechoma lagopsis is classified in the kingdom Plantae, and more specifically in the family Lamiaceae. This family is commonly known as the mint family, to which many other well-known herbs also belong. Within the family Lamiaceae, Glechoma lagopsis is placed in the genus Glechoma. The genus Glechoma is further divided into several species, with Glechoma lagopsis being one of them. Glechoma lagopsis is also known as creeping speedwell or gill-over-the-ground.
uses: Glechoma lagopsis, also known as blue spurrey or creeping speedwell, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Traditionally, it has been employed to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds. The plant is believed to possess antiseptic properties, which can help in reducing inflammation and fighting infections. In addition, it has been used to treat digestive problems, such as indigestion and bloating. Further research is necessary to confirm the efficacy and safety of Glechoma lagopsis as a medicinal herb.

side_effects: Glechoma lagopsis is also known as catmint or blue curls, but it's worth noting that Glechoma lagopsis is not to be confused with the more commonly known catmint, Nepeta cataria. However, I found some information about Glechoma hederacea, which is sometimes confused with Glechoma lagopsis, and it is said to have side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions in some individuals.
